coredata.dc:title: Functional carbon dots-hydrogel complex for selective antibacterial and detection applications
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Dan
Liu, Huan
Xu, Mengyu
Yin, Chengxin
Xiao, Xincai
Dai, Kang
coredata.dc:description: The carbon dots (CDs) with excellent optical properties and their hydrogel complex are of great significance in biomedicine, healthcare and biochemical detection fields. This paper reports the preparation of green-emitting CDs (MA-CDs) through one-step hydrothermal route with citric acid as reducing agent, L-malic acid as carbon source and N-(2-hydroxyethyl)ethylenediamine as nitrogen source. To expand its application in biology, MA-CDs were coupled with vancomycin to obtain multifunctional CDs (VMA-CDs). The prepared VMA-CDs exhibit selective antibacterial behavior to Gram-positive bacteria, and it could be used as a fluorescent probe to selectively label Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus). Moreover, thanks to the excellent optical properties of VMA-CDs, it has been used as a fluorescent sensor to detect Au3+ with detection range of 6.50 nM–21.93 μM and detection limit 3.98 nM. By introducing the fluorescence of CDs as the reference signal, and VMA-CDs as a response signal, the hydrogel (V-SP) was prepared and realized the detection of Au3+ in microfluidics with assistance of a smartphone to collect and analyze data.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots complex
Fluorescent hydrogel
Selective antibacterial
Imaging of Gram-positive bacteria
Detection of Au3+

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis method of MA-CDs and VMA-CDs
VMA-CDs as probes for the detection of Au3+
Visual detection of Au3+ by V-SP composite hydrogel
Synthesis and characterization of MA-CDs and VMA-CDs
The detection of Au3+ through the fluorescence quenching of CDs
The preparation of CDs-hydrogel composite and its application
The measurement of V-SP hydrogel to Au3+

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was funded by the Fundamental Research Funds for South-Central Minzu University (CZY22003) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (2197832).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–vis spectra and PL emission of (a) MA-CDs and (b) VMA-CDs, inset images of 10 mg/mL CDs solutions in daylight and UV light at 365 nm. (c) UV–vis spectra of VAN, MA-CDs and VMA-CDs. Fluorescence intensity of (d) MA-CDs and (e) VMA-CDs under different concentrations.

TEM images of (a) MA-CDs and (b) VMA-CDs, with the lattice image of CDs in the upper left inset and the histogram of the particle size distribution of CDs in the lower right, the scale is 10 nm. (c) FT-IR spectra of MA-CDs and VMA-CDs. (d) Zeta potential of MA-CDs and VMA-CDs. (e) XPS spectrum of MA-CDs and VMA-CDs. (f) XRD pattern of MA-CDs and VMA-CDs.
SEM images of S. aureus after treatment with (a) PBS and (b) VMA-CDs (100 μg/mL).

(a) Confocal images of E. coli in bright field, 488 and 405 nm excitation under incubation with VMA-CDs. (b) Confocal images of S. aureus in bright field, 488 and 405 nm excitation under incubation with VMA-CDs, the scale is 10 μm.

(a) Fluorescence response of VMA-CDs to different metal ions with ion concentration of 0.01 mM. (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of VMA-CDs in the presence of increasing Au3+ concentration (0–21.93 µM), inset shows the linear relationship between (F0-Fi)/F0 and Au3+.
UV–vis spectra of (a) MA-CDs and (b) VMA-CDs before and after addition of Au3+. TEM images of (c) MA-CDs and (d) VMA-CDs after addition of Au3+, the scale is 50 nm.
Pictures of (a) SP and (b) V-SP under fluorescent light. Pictures of (c) SP and (d) V-SP under UV lamp.

(a) Linearity of the ratio of the fluorescence intensity of V-SP soaked with different concentrations of Au3+ solution to that of V-SP not soaked with Au3+ versus the logarithm of Au3+ concentration in the linear range of 0.0002–400 μM, and the picture of V-SP under 365 nm UV lamp is shown on the right. (b) Linearity of the grayscale value of fluorescence intensity of V-SP soaked by different concentrations of Au3+ solution versus the logarithm of Au3+ concentration, the picture on the right is the grayscale picture of V-SP.
The antimicrobial ability of different CDs or VAN for different bacteria.
Inhibit bacterial circles’ size of subjects content for three times.

head.ce:title: Functional carbon dots-hydrogel complex for selective antibacterial and detection applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, South-Central Minzu University, Wuhan 430074, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, South-Central Minzu University, Wuhan 430074, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Demonstration Center for Experimental Ethnopharmacology Education (South-Central Minzu University), Wuhan 430065, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Demonstration Center for Experimental Ethnopharmacology Education (South-Central Minzu University)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Demonstration Center for Experimental Ethnopharmacology Education (South-Central Minzu University), Wuhan 430065, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, South-Central Minzu University, Wuhan 430074, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The carbon dots (CDs) with excellent optical properties and their hydrogel complex are of great significance in biomedicine, healthcare and biochemical detection fields. This paper reports the preparation of green-emitting CDs (MA-CDs) through one-step hydrothermal route with citric acid as reducing agent, L-malic acid as carbon source and N-(2-hydroxyethyl)ethylenediamine as nitrogen source. To expand its application in biology, MA-CDs were coupled with vancomycin to obtain multifunctional CDs (VMA-CDs). The prepared VMA-CDs exhibit selective antibacterial behavior to Gram-positive bacteria, and it could be used as a fluorescent probe to selectively label Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus). Moreover, thanks to the excellent optical properties of VMA-CDs, it has been used as a fluorescent sensor to detect Au3+ with detection range of 6.50 nM–21.93 μM and detection limit 3.98 nM. By introducing the fluorescence of CDs as the reference signal, and VMA-CDs as a response signal, the hydrogel (V-SP) was prepared and realized the detection of Au3+ in microfluidics with assistance of a smartphone to collect and analyze data.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As a zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted increasing attention thanks to their unique optical property, ease of modification and functionalization, and excellent biocompatibility [1,2]. The rich existence of functional groups on CDs surfaces allows easy modification and functionalization. Therefore, CDs have been more frequently endowed with other functions, including antimicrobial as well as detection, by modifying their surface [3,4]. Hydrogels are three-dimensional (3D) networks composed of physically or chemically cross-linked natural or synthetic polymers, and CDs could be embedded into hydrogels to prepare fluorescent hydrogels, for better mechanical properties, like good strength, toughness, and elasticity. It could extend the applications of common hydrogels into more specific fields, such as biomedicine and biosensing [5], making it a promising composite material for biomedical applications [6]. The researchers are now working to developing functional CDs complex and novel hydrogel to expand their applications in biomedical and other fields.

Bacterial infections are one of the most serious health problems in the world [7,8]. Vancomycin (VAN) is considered to be one of the glycopeptide antibiotics widely used for the treatment of various bacterial infections [9,10]. VAN-modified CDs could keep the specific optical properties and excellent biocompatibility of CDs, and also inherit the biochemical ability of VAN, make it the base for the novel complex to construct imaging/treating integrated platform. Since antibacterial CDs usually work in liquid phase, they could not stay in certain place for long time, limiting their application in biochemical fields. However, the prepared various types of CDs-embedded hydrogels could solve the problem while maintaining the bioactivity of CDs [11,12]. Shivam et. al [13] developed graphene quantum dot (GQD)-polyacrylic acid (PAA) hybrid hydrogel, which exhibited lower pro-inflammatory cytokines, interleukin (IL-6) and tumor necrosis factor (TNF-α) and higher expression of anti-inflammatory (IL-10) ability.

Fluorescent detection is one of the most important applications of CDs, attributing to the good selectivity, high sensitivity and quick response when CDs work as fluorescent sensors [14]. In particular, the combination of CDs and portable hydrogels allows direct and convenient detection of the targets. Zhao et al. [15] prepared low toxic red-fluorescent N,P-CDs with pH dependence by one-step hydrothermal method with o-phenylenediamine and phosphoric acid as raw materials, and further established a simple and fast sensing strategy to pH and malachite green (MG). The group combined the prepared N,P-CDs with hydrogel and developed a fluorescent hydrogel kit for the visual analysis of MG. As a common precious metal, gold is popular worldwide [16], but the ionic Au3+ shows potential toxicity to humans [17] and could firmly combine with DNA and several enzymes, leading to severe damage to the peripheral nervous system, liver, and kidneys [18]. Therefore, the monitoring of Au3+ pollution has attracted increasing interest from researchers [19]. The development of fluorescent hydrogel as probes, with the assistance of a smartphone, to establish portable in situ Au3+ detection systems is of great research value.

This paper reports the rapid synthesis of green-emitting MA-CDs through one-step hydrothermal route with L-malic acid (L-MA) as raw material. The prepared CDs were further coupled with VAN to obtain VMA-CDs with excellent fluorescence and antibacterial activity, and the product has been characterized through multiple means. VMA-CDs show selective antibacterial behavior to Gram-positive (G+) bacteria, with minimum inhibition concentration against S. aureus at 3.9 μg/mL, and could realize selective imaging to G+ bacteria as fluorescent probes. VMA-CDs exhibit high selectivity and sensitivity to Au3+ with a detection limit of 3.98 nM. Furthermore, the prepared CDs-hydrogel complex retained the optical and antibacterial ability of CDs, and it was also portable enough to be developed into a detection platform with the assistance of smartphones.

L-malic acid (L-MA), sodium alginate (SA), and vancomycin (VAN) were purchased from Source Leaf Biologicals. N-(2-hydroxyethyl)ethylenediamine (AEEA) were obtained from Aladdin (Shanghai) Co. Ltd.. Citric acid monohydrate (CA, >99.5 %), polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd.. 1-Ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)carbodiimide (EDC) hydrochloride and N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were purchased from Yuanye Biological Technology Co., Ltd.. Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus, CCTCC AB 91093), Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA, CCTCC AB 2015107), Bacillus subtilis (B. subtilis, CCTCC AB 90008) and Escherichia coli (E. coli, ATCC 25922) were provided by the China Typical Culture Collection Center.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis method of MA-CDs and VMA-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The green fluorescent MA-CDs were prepared through one-step hydrothermal route 0.3 g CA and 0.4 g L-MA were dissolved into 20 mL water, and 0.3 g AEEA was added. The solution was stirred for 10 min under N2 environment. The mixed solution was transferred to a 25 mL PTFE stainless steel autoclave and reacted at 200 °C for 4 h. The obtained yellow solution was centrifuged using a 1000 D ultrafiltration tube for 30 min to remove the unreacted raw materials. The solution was freeze-dried to obtain solid MA-CDs. A solution of 50 mg/mL of MA-CDs was prepared, and 0.1 g of EDC/NHS was added into the solution and stirred N2 for 1 h. 100 mg of VAN was added and stirred under N2 protection for 24 h. The obtained liquid was ultrafiltered with 3000 D ultrafiltration tube for 15 min to remove unreacted small molecules, and the obtained liquid was freeze-dried to obtain VMA-CDs.

1 mL of S. aureus or E. coli at a concentration of 109 CFU/mL was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 min, and the supernatant was removed and incubated with 1 mL of 80 μg/mL VMA-CDs at 37 °C for 3 h. After incubation, the incubation was washed 1–2 times and images were collected under a confocal microscope using a 60 × objective.

SP gel was prepared by the freezing method. The specific operation is as follows: 10 % PVA solution and 1 % SA solution are prepared separately, and the two are mixed well in a certain ratio, and then defoamed and injected into the mold. The empty SP solid gel was obtained by repeated freeze-thawing three times at −20 °C. The gels containing VMA-CDs were prepared the same as above, and different concentrations of VMA-CDs were added to the well-mixed SA and PVA, and the V-SP solid gels were obtained after repeated freeze-thawing.

S. aureus was diluted to obtain a bacterial count of 107 CFU/mL, and 100 μL drops were taken into a solid medium and evenly coated. The prepared V-SP with different concentrations (diameter 8 mm) were placed on the solid medium coated with bacteria and incubated at 37 °C for 12 h, then the diameter of the inhibition zone was measured and the results were repeated three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: VMA-CDs as probes for the detection of Au3+
ce:section.ce:para: VMA-CDs (1 mg/mL) were added to the quartz cell, and different metal ion solutions (Mn2+, Ag+, Fe3+, Zn2+, Na+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Co2+, Yb3+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Au3+) were added to the cell. After mixing well and standing for 3 min, the change of fluorescence intensity of the solution was recorded at 480 nm (λex = 400 nm). After measurement, it was found that the fluorescence of CDs was burst only when Au3+ was added, indicating that both prepared CDs could specifically detect Au3+.

To investigate the detection range of VMA-CDs solution (1 mg/mL) to the existence of Au3+, different concentrations of Au3+ solution were added to 1 mL of quartz cell and mixed thoroughly before the measurement. Fluorescence spectra were obtained after 3 min of reaction under excitation wavelength at 400 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual detection of Au3+ by V-SP composite hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared V-SP composite hydrogels were immersed in different concentrations of Au3+ solution for 5 min, and then the gels were removed and the surface water was blotted out. Their fluorescence intensities were measured using a fluorometer, and their images were photographed with a smartphone under a 365 nm UV lamp. The images’ grayscales were processed by Photoshop.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of MA-CDs and VMA-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: With CA as reducing agent, L-MA as carbon source, AEEA as nitrogen source with water as solvent (200 °C, 4 h), the green-emitting MA-CDs were prepared through one-step hydrothermal route. Fluorescent and UV–vis spectrology were used to study the basic optical properties of prepared MA-CDs (Fig. 1 (a)). The absorption peak of MA-CDs at 350 nm is attributed to the n-π* transition of the C=O bond [20]. Then the photoluminescence properties of MA-CDs at different concentrations were investigated. As shown Fig. 1(d), the fluorescence of MA-CDs exhibits concentration dependence. When MA-CDs concentration increases from 0.01 mg/mL to 10 mg/mL, the emission wavelength redshifts from 440 nm to 510 nm. The fluorescence intensity reaches maximum when MA-CDs concentration at 1 mg/mL.

To endow the biochemical ability of prepared MA-CDs, the classic EDC/NHS method was used to combine VAN with MA-CDs to acquire biologically competent VMA-CDs. As shown in Fig. 1(b) and (c), an absorption peak exits at 280 nm in the UV–vis spectrum of VAN, which is the characteristic peak of VAN. Comparatively, the same characteristic peak appears in the spectrum of VMA-CDs, indicating the successful coupling of VAN and MA-CDs. Similar to MA-CDs, VMA-CDs also exhibit fluorescence dependence with concentration. The increase of concentration (0.01 mg/mL–10 mg/mL) also leads to redshift of fluorescence emission wavelength (from 440 nm to 490 nm) (Fig. 1(e)). However, even at the same concentration, compared with MA-CDs, VMA-CDs’ emission wavelength is shorter and its fluorescence intensity is weaker. The fluorescence reached maximum when VMA-CDs concentration is at 3 mg/mL. The quantum yield of prepared VMA-CDs reached 28 %, much lower than that of MA-CDs (46 %).

The particle size, surface groups and surface charge all have great impacts upon the biochemical functions of prepared CDs. TEM, FT-IR and Zeta potential have been used to investigate the morphology, particle diameter, distribution, surface functional groups and surface charge of CDs. As shown in Fig. 2 (a), the prepared MA-CDs were spherical, well-distributed, and uniform in size, with an average particle diameter of 3.27 nm. As shown in Fig. 2(b), the average particle size of VMA-CDs after MA-CDs coupling with VAN is 3.72 nm. The increased particle size further proves the success of coupling. According to HRTEM images (top-left insets of Fig. 2(a) and (b)), the lattice spacing of MA-CDs is 0.34 nm, corresponding to the (002) plane of graphite [21], while the lattice spacing of VMA-CDs is 0.32 nm [22], also corresponding to the same plane. FT-IR has been used to characterize the prepared CDs. As shown in Fig. 2(c), these two acquired CDs exhibit the same absorption peaks at 1647, 3380 and 1560 cm−1, corresponding to the vibrations of C=O, –OH/–NH and N–H/C=C. The peaks at 2950 cm−1 and 1390 cm−1 are the peaks of –CH3 and –CH2, while the peaks at 1779 and 1708 cm−1 prove the presence of C=O. The peaks at 1487, 1080, and 1250 cm−1 represent the C-N=, C-O, and C-N vibrations, and the peaks at 1016, 816, and 997 cm−1 represent the stretching vibration of C-O-C. In addition, the VMA-CDs shows a vibrational absorption peak of C-Cl at 713 cm−1 [23], proving the successful coupling between VAN and MA-CDs. Under the same concentration, the Zeta potential of MA-CDs is + 4.09 mV, while that of VMA-CDs is + 7.78 mV (Fig. 2(d)). The increased charge promised easy combine with negatively-charged bacteria, beneficial to its inhibition behavior.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) has been used to analyze the functional groups of VMA-CDs and MA-CDs. Fig. 2(e) shows the complete XPS spectra of VMA-CDs and MA-CDs. MA-CDs mainly contains three elements: C (61.18 %), O (29.17 %) and N (9.65 %). The C 1 s spectrum of MA-CDs has three fitting peaks at 285.11 eV, 286.64 eV, and 288.91 eV (Fig. S1(a)), corresponding to C–C/C=C, C-N/C-O and C=O [24,25], while the two peaks of N 1 s at 400.54 eV and 401.85 eV (Fig. S1(b)), representing C-N–H (pyrrole N), -N(C)3 (graphite N) [26]. As shown in Fig. S1(c), the three fitting peaks of O 1 s spectrum prove the presence of C=O (531.83 eV) and C-OH/C-O-C (532.89 eV) [27]. In contrast, VMA-CDs contains four elements: C (62.35 %), O (23.07 %), N (12.60 %) and Cl (1.98 %), further proving the successful coupling of VAN with MA-CDs. As shown in Fig. S1(d), the Cl 2p spectrum is divided into four peaks at 197.75 eV (Cl 2p3/2chloride), 199.35 eV (Cl 2p1/2chloride), 200.08 eV (Cl 2p3/2C-Cl) and 202.37 eV (Cl 2p1/2C-Cl) [28].

To further determine the crystalline natures of VMA-CDs and MA-CDs, their XRD spectra were recorded. As shown in Fig. 2(f), MA-CDs exhibit a broad peak near 2θ = 22.5°, indicating the formation of amorphous carbon phase due to the introduction of nitrogen and oxygen containing groups [29]. VMA-CDs show a characteristic graphite peak at 2θ = 24.8°, corresponding to the (002) plate [30].

S. aureus, MRSA, and B. subtilis as representatives of G+ bacteria and E. coli as representative of Gram-negative (G−) bacteria have been selected as targets to test the biological activities of prepared CDs. As shown in Table 1 , the MIC values of MA-CDs against both G+ and G− bacteria are higher than 1 mg/mL, while VMA-CDs exhibited varied inhibitory abilities to different bacteria. The MIC against S. aureus, MRSA, and B. subtilis were 3.9, 7.8 and 2.0 μg/mL respectively, showing excellent antibacterial ability. Similar to that of VMA-CDs, VAN also shows different inhibiting ability to different G+ bacteria, which is the result of their difference in the thickness of bacterial cell wall. B. subtilis contains an inner wall region, Gram-positive cytoplasm and an outer wall region with thickness at 33 nm [31]. In contrast, the thickness of S. aureus cell wall is approximately 20–35 nm [32,33]. The references showed that the increased cell wall thickness would reduce the sensitivity of bacteria to the presence of VAN [34], which can thus explain the varied antibacterial effect of VMA-CDS/VAN to different G+ bacteria.

SEM was used to observe the impacts of VMA-CDs (100 μg/mL) upon the morphology of S. aureus cells. As shown in Fig. 3 (a), the cells after the treatment of PBS are in spherical shape, with smooth and complete surfaces, and some newly divided bacteria could be observed. The S. aureus cells, after the treatment of VMA-CDs, show pores and rupture on the surface with great morphological change, showing the severe damage of the bacteria (Fig. 3(b)). This proves that VMA-CDs kill bacteria through rupturing bacterial cells.

Fluorescence imaging has become a fast and simple tool for sensitive and low-cost visual bioanalysis. VMA-CDs possess excellent optical properties and could be used as fluorescent probes to label bacteria. S. aureus and E. coli were cultured with the presence of VMA-CDs, and observed under the confocal fluorescence microscope. As shown in Fig. 4 (a), after the incubation with the presence of VMA-CDs for 3 h, E. coli does not show fluorescence under the excitations of 405 and 488 nm, illustrating that VMA-CDs couldn’t interact with bacteria. While for S. aureus, after being incubated with VMA-CDs, it emitted blue and green fluorescence under the excitation of 405 and 488 nm (Fig. 4(b)). As shown in Fig. S2, the sole presence of S. aureus and E. coli could not emit any fluorescence under the excitation, so the appearance of fluorescence proves the successful combination between VMA-CDs and S. aureus. VAN on the surface of CDs is sensitive to G+ bacteria based on the combination with bacteria through interacting with peptidoglycan peptide terminus d-Ala-d-Ala in cell wall precursors [35]. The result proved that even at low concentrations, VMA-CDs could realize selective imaging of S. aureus, and could be used as fluorescent probe to label target bacteria.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The detection of Au3+ through the fluorescence quenching of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the responses of VMA-CDs to different metal ions, the fluorescence intensities of VMA-CDs with the presence of various ions (Mn2+, Ag+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Na+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Co2+, Yb3+, Cr3+, Fe3+ and Au3+) have been investigated. As shown from experiments, Au3+ shows the strongest quenching effect on the fluorescence of VMA-CDs, with a quenching rate of 79.5% (Fig. 5 (a)), while it could only quench 43.4% of MA-CDs fluorescence (Fig. S3), indicating high selectivity of VMA-CDs to Au3+ and can be used as a fluorescent probe for the detection of Au3+.

As shown in Fig. 5(b), with the increase in Au3+ concentration, the fluorescence intensity of VMA-CDs gradually drops, exhibiting a good linear relationship ((F0-Fi)/F0 = 0.0381 x + 0.141), with R2 = 0.9909, detection range at 6.50 nM–21.93 μM and limit of detection (LOD) at 3.98 nM. The UV–vis spectra and TEM images of CDs before and after the addition of Au3+ to explore the quenching mechanism of Au3+ to the fluorescence of CDs. The UV–vis spectra showed that, with the addition of Au3+, there appeared broad band at around 500–800 nm, and a shoulder peak at 400 nm (Fig. 6 (a) and (b)). The CDs solution changed from colorless to slightly colored. The TEM images also showed the impact of Au3+ upon the aggregation of CDs and the definition of TEM images. As shown in Fig. S4(a) and (b), initially the image of CDs could not be distinguished from background noise, which was because the major elements in CDs were C, H, O and N, leading to low contrast degree. While after the addition of Au3+, the particle definition and contrast degree enhanced, and the image exhibited clear aggregation of CDs, with larger particles (Fig. 6(c) and (d)).

Based on the results of UV–vis and TEM images, we proposed the quenching mechanism of Au3+ to the fluorescence of CDs. On one hand, the rich presence of N and O-containing functional groups, like carboxy, hydroxy and amino groups, allows CDs easily to aggregate with Au3+ [36], leading to the fluorescence quenching of CDs. Concerning about Au3+ higher efficiency to the fluorescence of VMA-CDs than to that of MA-CDs, the most reasonable one is that the larger size of VMA-CDs after CDs’ coupling with VAN is more beneficial to the interaction between Au3+ and the functional groups on CDs’ surface. In addition, the TEM images showed that with the presence of Au3+, VMA-CDs exhibited higher aggregation degree than MA-CDs, and this high extent of aggregation also leads to stronger quenching((Fig. 6(c) and (d)). For these reasons, Au3+ exhibits higher quenching efficiency to the fluorescence of VMA-CDs than that of MA-CDs.

On the other side, the strong reducing material CA as the raw materials of prepared CDs would leave residues on the surface of CDs, making them certain degree of reducibility, which is similar to the CDs with reducibility reported in some references [37]. As shown in Fig. 6(a) and (b), there is a shoulder peak at 400 nm in the UV–vis spectra of CDs. We infer that is the result of the aggregation of CDs. Because of the reducibility of CDs, CDs can reduce gold ions into small gold particles, which can be proven by the broad absorption band at around 500–800 nm. Some references also claimed that the broad band at long-wavelength area is attributed to the coupling of plasma bands on the surface of AuNPs [38]. Therefore, the formation of gold particles leads to the fluorescence quenching of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The preparation of CDs-hydrogel composite and its application
ce:section.ce:para: PVA hydrogels are an important synthetic polymeric hydrogel material with high chemical stability, good biocompatibility, and low toxicity [39]. SA is a widely used natural polymer with high hydrophilicity, biocompatibility, low toxicity, and low cost, and is often used in blends with PVA to prepare gels to overcome the limitations of PVA. PVA has a large number of hydroxyl groups on its side chains, which interact with SA and forms network structure through van der Waals forces and hydrogen bonds during repeated freeze-thawing [40]. VMA-CDs enter into the gel structure by encapsulation during gel preparation. As shown in Fig. 7 , the gel shows distinct blue fluorescence under 365 nm excitation.

Thanks to the strong antibacterial activity of prepared VMA-CDs to S. aureus, the antibacterial abilities of different hydrogels have been investigated through the inhibition ring tests. Different-concentrationed VMA-CDs were dropped into SP hydrogel to obtain V-SP of different concentrations. The inhibition zones on the plates were measured after the bacteria were cultured for 18 h with the existence of the material, to investigate the inhibitory abilities of the materials (Fig. S5). The experiment result showed (Table 2 ) the inhibition zone appeared with the presence of V-SP at the concentration of 8 μg/mL, showing the release of VMA-CDs from hydrogel to realize the antibacterial purpose. However, V-SP didn’t exhibit an inhibitory effect on G− bacteria, proving the selective antibacterial behavior to G+ bacteria. Freeze-thaw cycling method was used to prepare SP hydrogel. The gel has a variety of useful properties such as excellent biocompatibility, good biodegradability, high hydrophilicity, and excellent mechanical properties of the gel, making it possible to be used as drug-carrying material to deliver drugs. When the gel undergoes swelling, the pore size of the gel becomes larger and more drug can be loaded or released [41]. However, this swelling behavior can be affected by several factors, including polymer composition, crosslink density, pH, temperature, and solvent properties [42]. The prepared V-SP gel was set at 4 °C in the refrigerator. When used for antibacterial tests, the gel was set at the temperature that is beneficial for the growth of bacteria (37 °C). Under this temperature, the pore size of V-SP gel enlarged, and slowly released VMA-CDs to realize antibacterial purpose.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The measurement of V-SP hydrogel to Au3+
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescent intensities of V-SP hydrogel were tested after being soaked in Au3+ solution at different concentrations. It was found that in the range of 0.0002–400 μM (Fig. 8 (a)), the fluorescent ratio kept a good linear relationship with -LgCAu 3+ (y = 0.1156 x - 0.2738, R2 = 0.9964, I/I0 is the ratio of fluorescence intensities of hydrogel treated by Au3+ solution and blank hydrogel). Compared with V-SP, the undoped M−SP can also detect the existence of Au3+, but with a narrower detection range (Fig. S6).

To realize the real-time detection of Au3+ in the field, the grayscale values of the hydrogel were measured with the assistance of a digital camera and simple software processing, to acquire the content of Au3+. As shown in Fig. 8(b), the ratio of the grayscale value of V-SP (containing Au3+) to the grayscale value of the blank V-SP is in good linearity with -LgCAu3+ in the range of 0.0002–400 μM, indicating the realization of rapid detection through smartphones, further expanding the application of CDs and their complexes.

This paper reports the preparation of green luminescent MA-CDs from L-MA by a one-step hydrothermal method, and products were further coupled with VAN to obtain VMA-CDs, which exhibited excellent fluorescence performance and strong bioactivity. As a glycopeptide antibiotic agent, VAN could inhibit the formation of bacterial cell walls of G+ bacteria, whose main component is peptidoglycan, and finally realize the antibacterial effect. The VMA-CDs complex inherited the selective antibacterial ability of G+ bacteria from VAN, and realized the selective cell imaging to G+ and G− bacteria. The complex also shows good detection ability to Au3+, with a detection limit at 3.98 nM, and linear range at 6.5 nM–21.93 μM. Furthermore, the prepared VMA-CDs were coupled with hydrogel, to obtain V-SP hydrogel with good portability and mechanic properties, and the hydrogel also shows selective antibacterial ability to G+ bacteria. The V-SP gel was further used to realize the detection of Au3+ with the assistance of a smartphone and simple software processing.

Dan Zhao: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition. Huan Liu: Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Mengyu Xu: Investigation. Chengxin Yin: Data curation. Xincai Xiao: Validation, Conceptualization. Kang Dai: Software, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was funded by the Fundamental Research Funds for South-Central Minzu University (CZY22003) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (2197832).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.124195.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
